text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

text:
Welcome participants to the Missing Voices in Data lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Missing voices analysis asks whose experiences, records, language needs, disability access needs, rural context, tribal sovereignty interests, or community knowledge are absent from the data.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Missing voices analysis asks whose experiences, records, language needs, disability access needs, rural context, tribal sovereignty interests, or community knowledge are absent from the data.

text: Missing voices analysis helps agencies avoid designing AI systems around only the data and perspectives that are easiest to obtain. It is essential for equity, trust, and real-world performance.
textPreview: Missing voices analysis helps agencies avoid designing AI systems around only the data and perspectives that are easiest to obtain. It is...

text: Models may appear accurate overall while failing communities that are missing, underrepresented, misclassified, or poorly represented.
textPreview: Models may appear accurate overall while failing communities that are missing, underrepresented, misclassified, or poorly represented.

Use this slide in the Missing Voices in Data training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 4, 7, 13 and Tools 3, 22, 47.
Emphasize the core guardrail: Models may appear accurate overall while failing communities that are missing, underrepresented, misclassified, or poorly represented.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

• Explain Missing Voices in Data in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Missing Voices in Data training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 4, 7, 13 and Tools 3, 22, 47.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

Missing voices analysis asks who is absent from the data, design process, validation testing, governance discussion, or evaluation. Public health AI can look accurate overall while failing groups that are underrepresented, misclassified, or affected differently by the workflow. Missing voices review should happen before use case approval and again during monitoring.

text: Definition: Missing voices analysis identifies people, communities, settings, and forms of knowledge that are absent or poorly represented in the data, design process, validation evidence, or monitoring plan.

People with limited English proficiency, people with disabilities, rural residents, people experiencing homelessness, immigrant. Frontline staff who understand workflow realities but are not included in planning. Community partners who can.

For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?

Whose data, experience, language needs, disability access needs, rural context, or community knowledge may be missing? Who should validate assumptions before launch? How will subgroup performance and community concerns be monitored after.
